1: The word-event formula introduces the extended ship-allegory — one of the most elaborate extended metaphors in the prophetic literature. Ch 27 is simultaneously a dirge (qinah) and a geographical-commercial catalog, providing the most detailed single description of ancient Near Eastern trade networks outside of administrative cuneiform records.
2: 'Raise a lamentation over Tyre' — the qinah form: the 3:2 meter of Hebrew funeral poetry applied to a city. This is one of three formal lamentations over Tyre in Ezekiel 26-28 (Ezekiel 26:17-18 being the first, Ezekiel 28:12-19 the third). The cumulative effect of multiple lamentations over the same city is unusual and reflects the extraordinary prominence of Tyre in the ancient world — no other non-Israelite city receives this degree of sustained elegiac attention.
3: 'You who are situated at the gateway of the sea, merchant to many coastlands' — Tyre's geographical identity: at the interface of the eastern Mediterranean and the land routes of the Levant. The term 'gateway of the sea' (pethach hayammim) captures Tyre's commercial position: the sea routes from the west (Spain, North Africa, Greece, Cyprus) met the land routes from the east (Mesopotamia, Arabia, Egypt) at Tyre. 'Perfect in beauty' (kalil yophyi) — the aesthetic dimension of Tyre's self-understanding, which the king-oracle of Ezekiel 28 will then subvert.
4: 'Your builders have perfected your beauty' — the urban construction of Tyre as an aesthetic achievement. The island city required constant engineering: sea walls, harbor constructions, bridges to cisterns and water sources on the mainland, elaborate building projects within the cramped island space. Tyrian craftsmen were famous throughout the ancient Near East: they built Solomon's temple (1 Kgs 5-7) and provided artisans for numerous royal projects.
5: 'They made all your planks of fir trees from Senir' — Senir (H8149) is one of the names for Mount Hermon (cf. Deuteronomy 3:9: the Amorites called it Senir while the Sidonians called it Sirion). The fir trees (beroshim) from the Anti-Lebanon range were prized for shipbuilding — straight-grained, lightweight, and resistant to water. The cedars of Lebanon for the mast: cedar was the premier prestige timber of the ancient Near East, used for the finest construction. Tyre had direct access to the best shipbuilding timber in the world through its position at the foot of the Lebanon range.
6: 'Oaks of Bashan' for oars — the hard oak of the Bashan plateau (Golan Heights and northern Transjordan) was preferred for oars due to its strength and flexibility. 'Cypress wood inlaid with ivory from the coastlands of Cyprus' for the deck — Cyprus (Kittim) was both a source of copper and of fine cedar/cypress wood; its ivory came through trade with Syria and Egypt. The combination of multiple luxury materials in the deck construction reflects the extravagant craftsmanship associated with Phoenician woodwork.
7: 'Fine embroidered linen from Egypt' for the sail — Egyptian linen was the finest in the ancient world; the Amarna letters and Egyptian papyri document Phoenician purchases of Egyptian textiles. 'Blue and purple from the coastlands of Elishah' (Alashiya = Cyprus) for the awning/flag — purple dye (argaman) was Tyre's most famous product. Tyrian purple (from Murex marine snails) was the most expensive dye in antiquity; only royalty and the highest nobility could afford purple-dyed textiles. The purple-fishing industry and dyeing installations have been archaeologically confirmed in the Tyre area.
8: 'The inhabitants of Sidon and Arvad were your oarsmen' — Sidon (Tyre's sister/senior city, 40 km north) and Arvad (an island city at the northern end of the Phoenician coast, modern Arwad). The Phoenician cities supplied each other's labor needs and crew bases. 'Your own skilled men, O Tyre, served as your pilots' — the Tyrians themselves as navigators and captains of their own fleet, with the Sidonians and Arvadians as the skilled labor force of rowers.
9: 'The elders of Gebal and its skilled craftsmen were in you as caulkers' — Gebal is Byblos, the ancient Phoenician city (modern Jbeil, 40 km north of Beirut), one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world. The Bybliotes were famous as ship-builders and repairers; the caulking of seams with tar and fiber was essential maintenance for wooden vessels. 'All the ships of the sea with their sailors were in you to trade your merchandise' — Tyre as the global emporium: all the world's ships called at Tyre.
10: 'Persia, Lud, and Put served as soldiers in your army' — Tyre employed mercenary troops from far afield: Persia (Iran), Lud (possibly Lydia in western Anatolia or Lydians in general), and Put (Libya/Nubia). Phoenician city-states, like Greek poleis, relied heavily on mercenary soldiers rather than citizen armies. The ethnic diversity of the mercenary force reflects Tyre's commercial connections — far-flung trade relationships provided access to military specialists from across the known world.
11: The Arvadians and 'Gammadim' (gamadim, possibly mercenaries from Cilicia/southern Anatolia) manning the walls — the military defense of the island city was essential. The shields hung on the walls as decoration was a common ancient Near Eastern display of military readiness and trophies: 1 Kings 10:16-17 describes Solomon displaying 500 large gold shields in the House of the Forest of Lebanon. 'They made your beauty perfect' — the military display as aesthetic achievement, the armed power as part of the city's magnificence.
12: 'Tarshish was your merchant because of your abundant wealth of every kind; with silver, iron, tin, and lead they traded at your markets' — Tarshish (Spanish Tartessus, or possibly Sardinia) was Tyre's far-western trading partner for metals. The four metals listed (silver, iron, tin, lead) correspond to the metal resources of the Iberian Peninsula and Sardinian mines that ancient Phoenician colonization targeted. Tin was particularly critical: it was necessary for bronze production, and the Mediterranean world's only major tin sources in antiquity were in Spain (Rio Tinto area) and Cornwall, England.
13: 'Javan, Tubal, and Meshech were your traders; they traded human beings and vessels of bronze in your markets' — Javan (Greece/Ionian coast), Tubal and Meshech (Asia Minor, possibly Phrygia and Cappadocia) trading slaves (nephesh adam, 'souls of men' — human beings as a commodity) and bronze vessels. The slave trade was a significant component of ancient Near Eastern commerce, and Phoenician merchants were prominent in it (Joel 3:6 accuses the Phoenicians of selling Israelites to the Greeks). The bronze vessels from Anatolia were famous in antiquity; Greek bronze-working tradition had deep Anatolian roots.
14: 'From Beth-togarmah they traded horses, war horses, and mules at your markets' — Beth-Togarmah (Armenia/eastern Anatolia) as the horse-supply region. The horse trade from the north was vital for military establishments throughout the Near East; horses did not breed well in the lowlands and had to be imported from highland regions. War horses (susim, H5483) and 'horses of the parade/war' (paras, H6571, war horse/charger) were premium commodities; mules were standard pack animals and the preferred riding animals for nobility.
15: 'The men of Dedan were your traders' — Dedan (northern Arabia, modern al-'Ula area) as a caravan hub connecting the Arabian trade to the Mediterranean via Phoenician markets. 'Many coastlands were your markets' — Tyre as the intermediary for island and coastal trade. 'They brought you tribute of ivory tusks and ebony' — ivory from African elephants (via Arabia and Egypt) and ebony (African hardwood, H1894) were luxury goods in high demand for carved furniture, inlay work, and decorative objects throughout the ancient Near East.
16: 'Syria was your merchant because of your many products' — Syria (Aram) as a major trading partner. 'With emeralds, purple, embroidered work, fine linen, coral, and agate they traded at your markets' — the range of goods reflects Syria's position at the crossroads of Mesopotamian and Mediterranean trade. Coral (ra'moth, possibly red coral from the Red Sea), agate (kadkod, possibly crystal or carnelian) — gemstones from India and Arabia passing through Syrian middlemen.
17: 'Judah and the land of Israel were your traders; they traded wheat from Minnith, cakes, honey, olive oil, and balm at your markets' — the specific mention of Judah/Israel as Tyre's agricultural suppliers is historically significant: the Jezreel Valley's wheat, Canaan's olive oil and honey, and the famous Gilead balm (tzori, the medicinal resin of the Commiphora species, cf. Genesis 37:25; Jeremiah 8:22) all flowed to Tyre's markets. Minnith (H4511, a location east of the Jordan in Ammonite territory, cf. Judges 11:33) was a wheat-producing area. This verse shows the commercial relationship that made Israel's reliance on Tyre commercially rational and spiritually dangerous.
18: 'Damascus was your merchant for your many products because of your abundant wealth — with the wine of Helbon and white wool' — Damascus as the Aramean merchant center. Wine of Helbon (Chalybon, near Damascus): attested in cuneiform sources as a luxury wine supplied to Persian royal courts; the wine of Helbon/Aleppo region was famous throughout the ancient Near East. White wool (H6713, tzemer Chaver, some read 'wool of Sahar/Zahar' — a toponym) as a luxury textile raw material.
19: 'Dan and Javan from Uzal traded at your markets; wrought iron, cassia, and fragrant calamus were among your wares' — the trade in aromatics and spices that gave Phoenician merchants their distinctive place in Mediterranean commerce. Cassia (qiddah, H6916, a cinnamon-related spice from South Asia or East Africa) and fragrant calamus (qaneh hatov, sweet cane/calamus, also from South/Southeast Asia) were luxury aromatics used in incense, medicine, and perfumery. These goods arrived via the Arabia-overland route.
20: 'Dedan traded with you in saddlecloths for riding' — a second reference to Dedan (cf. Ezekiel 27:15), this time specifically for riding accessories. The Arabian horse-gear trade supplemented the Armenian horse supply: horses needed saddle cloths, bridles, and tack. Arabian craftsmen were known for their leather and textile work for equestrian equipment.
21: 'Arabia and all the princes of Kedar traded with you in lambs, rams, and goats' — the Arabian peninsula as the source of livestock for Tyre's markets. Kedar (H6938, the major confederation of north Arabian tribes mentioned in Assyrian records as Qidri) was the dominant pastoral power of the north Arabian steppe. The lambs and sheep supplied both wool/leather for Tyre's textile trade and animals for sacrificial use throughout the region's temples and festivals.
22: 'The merchants of Sheba and Raamah traded with you; they traded in the finest of all spices, all kinds of precious stones, and gold' — Sheba (southwestern Arabia, possibly modern Yemen) and Raamah (unidentified, possibly in the same region) as the luxury goods suppliers from the Arabian Peninsula. The trade route from southern Arabia (the 'Incense Route') brought frankincense, myrrh, spices, and gold from Arabia and East Africa to Mediterranean ports via Tyre. This is the same commercial network referenced when the Queen of Sheba visits Solomon (1 Kings 10:1-10).
23: 'Haran, Canneh, Eden, the traders of Sheba, Asshur, and Chilmad were your merchants' — the northern Mesopotamian trade corridor: Haran (the major caravan city in northern Mesopotamia, also Abraham's stopping point, Genesis 11:31), Canneh (possibly Calneh/Nippur in central Mesopotamia), Eden (possibly the bit-adini Aramean kingdom in the middle Euphrates region, known from Assyrian records). Asshur (Assyria) as a commercial partner even after Assyria's political fall — the infrastructure of Assyrian commerce survived the empire's collapse. This geographic sweep (from Spain/Tarshish in the west to Arabia/Sheba in the south to Mesopotamia/Asshur in the east) demonstrates Tyre's role as the commercial hub of the ancient world.
24: 'These traded with you in choice garments, in blue and embroidered cloth, in chests of fine clothing bound with cords and made of cedar' — the luxury textile trade in which Tyre both participated and set standards. 'Blue and embroidered cloth' (tekheleth and rikmah) — the same blue-violet dye and embroidered fabric used in the tabernacle furnishings (Exodus 26:1,31) points to the shared material culture of the Levantine luxury textile industry.
25: 'The ships of Tarshish carried your merchandise; you were filled and made very glorious in the heart of the seas' — the 'ships of Tarshish' (oniyot Tarshish) were the largest class of Mediterranean merchant vessels, capable of the long ocean voyage to Spain. They are attested in multiple ancient sources as Phoenician/Tyrian ships. Isaiah 23:1-14 is entirely devoted to the mourning of the ships of Tarshish at Tyre's fall. The phrase 'filled and made very glorious' (wattikbedi me'od belbab yamim) captures the paradox of the allegory: the ship's wealth was also its fatal heaviness when the storm came.
26: 'Your rowers have brought you out into the great waters; the east wind has wrecked you in the heart of the seas' — the east wind (ruach qadim, the sirocco/khamsin) coming from the Babylonian east. The nautical reversal: the same ships that carried Tyre's merchandise to the world are now wrecked. The 'heart of the seas' (lev yamim) is where Tyre sat in commercial glory (Ezekiel 27:4, 25) and now where it is wrecked — the same location transforms from the center of wealth to the place of destruction.
27: 'Your wealth, your merchandise, your goods, your mariners and pilots, your caulkers and dealers, all your men of war within you, and all your company that is in your midst will sink into the heart of the seas on the day of your fall' — the comprehensive list of everything that constituted Tyre's identity: commercial goods, the people who carried them (mariners, pilots, caulkers, traders), the military forces that defended them, all going down together. The catalog of Tyre's greatness (Ezekiel 27:3-25) becomes the catalog of total loss.
28: 'At the sound of the cry of your pilots the countryside shakes' — the sound that once represented Tyre's commercial authority (the pilots giving orders) now represents their distress cry. The shaking countryside (migrash, H4054, open land/suburbs) reverting to shock at the maritime world's collapse. The sound that structured the commerce of the ancient Mediterranean becomes the sound of lamentation.
29: 'All who handle the oar — the mariners and all the pilots of the sea — will come down from their ships and stand on dry land' — the reaction of the maritime world to Tyre's fall: sailors abandoning their ships, the professional mariners paralyzed with grief. The standing on dry land is a departure from their element — sailors who live at sea coming ashore in shock. The universality ('all who handle the oar') captures the scope of the commercial world's connection to Tyre.
30: 'They will raise their voices over you and cry out bitterly; they will throw dust on their heads and roll in ashes' — the formal mourning gestures of the ancient Near East: loud crying (za'aq), dust-throwing on the head, rolling in ashes. These are the same mourning gestures used for personal bereavement and for the fall of cities; the commercial partners of Tyre mourn its loss as a personal bereavement because their own livelihood depended on the Tyrian commercial network.
31: 'They will shave their heads completely for you and put on sackcloth; they will weep for you with bitter grief and bitter mourning' — the escalating mourning: shaved heads, sackcloth garments, bitter weeping. The formality of the mourning protocol indicates that the commercial partners' grief is genuine — not theatrical — because their economic existence was bound to Tyre. The fall of the commercial hub was literally catastrophic for those in its orbit.
32: 'Who was ever destroyed like Tyre, like one silent in the midst of the sea?' — the uniqueness of Tyre's fall: there was no precedent. 'Silent in the midst of the sea' (kedomah betoch hayam) — the noise of Tyre's commercial activity (songs, harps, v.Ezekiel 26:13; merchant cries, ship sounds) is replaced by silence. The sea that once rang with Tyre's commerce becomes Tyre's silent grave.
33: 'When your merchandise went out over the seas, you satisfied many peoples; with your great wealth and goods you enriched the kings of the earth' — the tribute to Tyre's genuine commercial contribution: the city that satisfied (save'at) many peoples and enriched kings was not merely predatory but genuinely productive. The commercial system Tyre operated fed people, enriched regions, distributed goods. This acknowledgment of genuine benefit makes the fall more poignant and establishes that what is being mourned is a real civilizational achievement.
34: 'Now you are wrecked by the seas, in the depths of the waters; your merchandise and all your company have sunk with you' — the completed destruction: the past tense of fulfillment. The entire commercial edifice (merchandise, company, sailors, goods) has gone down together. The depths of the water complete the image begun with Tyre 'in the heart of the seas' — the center of the world becomes the bottom of the world.
35: 'All the inhabitants of the coastlands are appalled at you; their kings shudder with horror and their faces are troubled' — the reaction of the maritime political world to Tyre's fall. 'Appalled' (shamemu, H8074) and 'shudder with horror' (sa'ar, H8175) are the specific reactions to theophanic or catastrophic events — normally reserved for the response to YHWH's direct intervention. The fall of Tyre produces reactions appropriate to a divine act, because it is a divine act.
36: 'The merchants among the peoples hiss at you; you have come to a horrible end and will be no more forever' — the hissing (sharaq, H8319, whistle/hiss) is a conventional response to destroyed cities in the OT prophetic literature (Jeremiah 19:8; Ezekiel 49:17; Ezekiel 50:13 — all using sharaq for the shocked reaction to ruined cities). 'Will be no more forever' (ayin ad olam) — the permanent dissolution of Tyre as a distinct commercial civilization closes the dirge.
